food_subgroup: Fermented rice and coconut milk bowl pancake

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific concept for Appam. Nearest parent is a fermented rice-coconut pancake. A new concept covering appam (bowl-shaped South Indian pancake) is warranted.
ontology.foodex2_description: Fermented rice-based pancake with coconut milk (closest parent)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of appam is 2 pieces (approximately 70–75g each, 150g total), each a thin lacy pancake with crispy edges and a soft spongy centre formed by cooking in a small appachatti (bowl-shaped pan).

nutrient_source.title: Appam — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05010001
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Exact match in HPB Foodo under food code F05010001 — Appam, fermented rice and coconut milk pancake, standard preparation.

gi_source: University of Sydney GI Database — estimated from fermented rice pancake category; GI is high due to white rice starch but fat from coconut milk moderates somewhat.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium approximately 263mg per 2-piece serving (150g) — moderate; coconut milk adds no sodium naturally.
hypertension.guidance: Suitable for hypertensive patients. Appam is naturally low in sodium. The accompaniments (sweetened coconut milk, vegetable stew) add minimal sodium compared to curry-based accompaniments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (~76) due to gelatinised white rice starch; glycaemic load per 2-piece serving is approximately 34.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Diabetic patients should limit appam to 1 piece and pair with egg or vegetable stew to increase protein content; avoid sweetened coconut milk accompaniment which adds further sugar.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Coconut milk provides medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs) with some evidence for anti-inflammatory effects; fermentation supports gut health.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Appam is generally suitable for patients with rheumatoid arthritis. The saturated fat from coconut should not be a concern at standard serving levels.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Appam is a beautifully distinctive South Indian — particularly Kerala — pancake made from fermented rice batter blended with coconut milk. Cooked in a small, rounded wok called an appachatti, the batter spreads to create a circular pancake with thin, lacy, crispy edges and a thick, soft, spongy centre. The coconut milk gives appam a subtle sweetness and rich aroma. It is gluten-free and vegan. In Singapore, appam is found at South Indian and Sri Lankan restaurants and is typically served with sweetened coconut milk, vegetable stew (ishtew), or egg.

confidence_reason: Exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo Database under food code F05010001 — Appam, standard preparation.
notes: Vegan and gluten-free. Coconut is classified as a tree nut allergen. Cholesterol is 0.0. The high saturated fat per 100g (~3.8g) is entirely from coconut milk — medium-chain triglycerides (MCTs). Accompaniments are not included.
